Problem Statement¶

Business Context¶

Understanding customer personality and behavior is pivotal for businesses to enhance customer satisfaction and increase revenue. Segmentation based on a customer's personality, demographics, and purchasing behavior allows companies to create tailored marketing campaigns, improve customer retention, and optimize product offerings.

A leading retail company with a rapidly growing customer base seeks to gain deeper insights into their customers' profiles. The company recognizes that understanding customer personalities, lifestyles, and purchasing habits can unlock significant opportunities for personalizing marketing strategies and creating loyalty programs. These insights can help address critical business challenges, such as improving the effectiveness of marketing campaigns, identifying high-value customer groups, and fostering long-term relationships with customers.

With the competition intensifying in the retail space, moving away from generic strategies to more targeted and personalized approaches is essential for sustaining a competitive edge.

Objective¶

In an effort to optimize marketing efficiency and enhance customer experience, the company has embarked on a mission to identify distinct customer segments. By understanding the characteristics, preferences, and behaviors of each group, the company aims to:

  1. Develop personalized marketing campaigns to increase conversion rates.
  2. Create effective retention strategies for high-value customers.
  3. Optimize resource allocation, such as inventory management, pricing strategies, and store layouts.

As a data scientist tasked with this project, your responsibility is to analyze the given customer data, apply machine learning techniques to segment the customer base, and provide actionable insights into the characteristics of each segment.

Data Dictionary¶

The dataset includes historical data on customer demographics, personality traits, and purchasing behaviors. Key attributes are:

  1. Customer Information

    • ID: Unique identifier for each customer.
    • Year_Birth: Customer's year of birth.
    • Education: Education level of the customer.
    • Marital_Status: Marital status of the customer.
    • Income: Yearly household income (in dollars).
    • Kidhome: Number of children in the household.
    • Teenhome: Number of teenagers in the household.
    • Dt_Customer: Date when the customer enrolled with the company.
    • Recency: Number of days since the customer’s last purchase.
    • Complain: Whether the customer complained in the last 2 years (1 for yes, 0 for no).

  2. Spending Information (Last 2 Years)

    • MntWines: Amount spent on wine.
    • MntFruits: Amount spent on fruits.
    • MntMeatProducts: Amount spent on meat.
    • MntFishProducts: Amount spent on fish.
    • MntSweetProducts: Amount spent on sweets.
    • MntGoldProds: Amount spent on gold products.

  3. Purchase and Campaign Interaction

    • NumDealsPurchases: Number of purchases made using a discount.
    • AcceptedCmp1: Response to the 1st campaign (1 for yes, 0 for no).
    • AcceptedCmp2: Response to the 2nd campaign (1 for yes, 0 for no).
    • AcceptedCmp3: Response to the 3rd campaign (1 for yes, 0 for no).
    • AcceptedCmp4: Response to the 4th campaign (1 for yes, 0 for no).
    • AcceptedCmp5: Response to the 5th campaign (1 for yes, 0 for no).
    • Response: Response to the last campaign (1 for yes, 0 for no).

  4. Shopping Behavior

    • NumWebPurchases: Number of purchases made through the company’s website.
    • NumCatalogPurchases: Number of purchases made using catalogs.
    • NumStorePurchases: Number of purchases made directly in stores.
    • NumWebVisitsMonth: Number of visits to the company’s website in the last month.

Let's start coding!¶

Importing necessary libraries¶

In [101]:
# Libraries to help with reading and manipulating data
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# libaries to help with data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Removes the limit for the number of displayed columns
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)
# Sets the limit for the number of displayed rows
pd.set_option("display.max_rows", 200)

# to scale the data using z-score
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# to compute distances
from scipy.spatial.distance import cdist, pdist

# to perform k-means clustering and compute silhouette scores
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score

# to visualize the elbow curve and silhouette scores
from yellowbrick.cluster import KElbowVisualizer, SilhouetteVisualizer

# to suppress warnings
import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

Loading the data¶

drive.mount('/content/drive')

Drive already mounted at /content/drive; to attempt to forcibly remount, call drive.mount("/content/drive", force_remount=True).
In [103]:
# loading data into a pandas dataframe
data = pd.read_csv("/content/drive/MyDrive/Data Science Coursework/Project 1/Customer_Personality_Segmentation.csv", sep="\t")

Majority of the columns are integers, income column appears to be a float and three object columns (Education, Marital_Status, Dt_Customer). From observation, Dt_Customer column should be adjusted to be a datetime datatype.

Observations: Change the datatype of Dt_Customer column and the income column appears to be missing some values (24 to be exact)¶

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Univariate Analysis¶

Question 5: Explore all the variables and provide observations on their distributions. (histograms and boxplots)¶

In [114]:
# Write your code here
##code to generate side-by-side histogram and boxplots for each column
for col in data_processed.columns:
    print(col)
    plt.figure(figsize=(15,4))
    plt.subplot(1,2,1)
    data_processed[col].hist(bins=10, grid=False)
    plt.ylabel('count')
    plt.subplot(1,2,2)
    sns.boxplot(x=data_processed[col])
    plt.show()

Year_Birth

Z_CostContact

Z_Revenue

Response
Observations:¶
  • Income, all spending information and number of purchases made using a discount are very heavily right-skewed. So are number of web and catalog purchases.
  • Majority of customers do not respond to all the campaigns. Those who respond are actually considered the 'outliers'.
  • Overwhelming majortiy of the customer base has a 'Graduation' level of Education.
  • Customer's year of birth seems to be the only feature that is relatively left-skewed.
  • Z_Revenue and Z_CostContact seem to all have the same value within their columns
  • Recency, Dt_Customer and ID are more uniformly distributed. The closest column to being normally distributed is numberof store purchases.

Based on above results, we can drop the ID, Z_CostContact and Z_Revenue and Education plus Marital_Status(string) columns

In [116]:
##code to to drop columns
data_processed.drop(columns = ['ID','Z_CostContact','Z_Revenue','Education', 'Marital_Status'], inplace = True)
data_processed.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>

In [117]:
# Write your code here
##create a heatmap to show correlation between all variables
plt.figure(figsize=(15,10))
sns.heatmap(data_processed.corr(), annot=True, fmt='0.2f')
plt.show()
Observations:¶
  • Income is positively correlated with all spending power which makes sense because the more money one has, the more they are willing to spend.
  • Number of visits to the company website in the last month is negatively correlated with income which in turn makes it negatively correlated with all spending information.
  • The most positive correlation is between the amount spent on meat and number of purchases made using catalogs.
  • An interesting one is the number of kids one has at home tends to negatively correlate with all spending information which might make sense because more kids probably means less of a disposable income.
  • Also, perishable products like fruits, fish and meat don't tend to be bought through the website as opposed to in store. This is a contrast from gold procuts which is a lot more on par between web and store purchase.
  • The spending information tend to correlate well with one another probably because customers do tend to buy items all at once.

K-means Clustering¶

Question 7 : Select the appropriate number of clusters using the elbow Plot. What do you think is the appropriate number of clusters?¶

In [120]:
##generate elbow plot
Elbow = {}

for k in range(1, 10):
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, max_iter=1000).fit(data_scaled)
    Elbow[k] = kmeans.inertia_

# step 3
plt.figure()
plt.plot(list(Elbow.keys()), list(Elbow.values()), 'bo-')
plt.xlabel("Number of cluster")
plt.ylabel("Sum of Squares")
plt.show()
Observations: 2 clusters seems to be ideal based on the elbow plot.¶

Question 8 : finalize appropriate number of clusters by checking the silhoutte score as well. Is the answer different from the elbow plot?¶

In [129]:
# Write your code here
## code to generate silhouette score
ss = {}

for k in range(2, 10):
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k).fit(data_scaled)
    labels = kmeans.predict(data_scaled)
    ss[k] = silhouette_score(data_scaled, labels)

#plot for silhouette score
plt.figure()
plt.plot(list(ss.keys()), list(ss.values()), 'bo-')
plt.xlabel("Number of cluster")
plt.ylabel("Silhouette Score")
plt.show()
Observations: My silhouette score seems to change everytime I compile the code for it but for this run it appears to have a k of 5 which does not match that of my elbow plot for WCSS.¶

Question 9: Do a final fit with the appropriate number of clusters. How much total time does it take for the model to fit the data?¶

In [131]:
# Write your code here
##run the k-means algorithm using cluster 2
data_scaled_copy = data_scaled
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=2, max_iter= 1000, random_state=1)
kmeans.fit(data_scaled)

#Adding predicted labels to the original data and scaled data
data_scaled_copy['Labels'] = kmeans.predict(data_scaled)
data['Labels'] = kmeans.predict(data_scaled)
In [133]:
##check for the distribution of clusters
data.Labels.value_counts()
Out[133]:
count
Labels
1 1351
0 889

Observations: Took less than a second to fit the data¶

Cluster Profiling and Comparison¶

Question 10: Perform cluster profiling using boxplots for the K-Means algorithm. Analyze key characteristics of each cluster and provide detailed observations.¶

In [138]:
# Write your code here
##create boxplots for each label
data_scaled.boxplot(by = 'Labels',layout = (1,23), figsize=(50,7))
plt.show()
Observations: I belive the core distribution of the company's customers can be separated into:¶
  • Customers who earn a lot - The company should probably try to incentivize them to be in the stores as they seem to make the most purchases across all spending categories and like being in the stores anyways
  • Customers who earn less - The company should try incentivizing them with online deal and discounts as they make the most use of discounts and seem to have made more use of the company's website in the last month. They also seem to usually have more kids which probably results in less 'wasteful' spending.

Question 11: Perform cluster profiling on the data using a barplot for the K-Means algorithm. Provide insights and key observations for each cluster based on the visual analysis.¶

In [142]:
# Write your code here
sns.countplot(data=data_scaled_copy, x='Labels', palette='rainbow');
plt.show()
Observations: Though the group that earns more contribute a lot more to the company's purchases, it is quite evident that the group earning less constitutes a larger majority of the customer base so an effort has to be made to help convert this number into more spending.¶

Business Recommedations¶

Question 12: Based on the cluster insights, what business recommendations can be provided?¶

The company has to help the larger majortiy of its customer base that earns less to spend more by offering online deals and discounts that might be enticing enough to result in purchases as this is the group that really needs their numbers to be converted into purchases. The group that earns more has to be incentivized to be in the store and a layout that allows different items from different categories to easily be within reach will be most likely ideal within the stores. This should be ideal because they seem to make purchases regardless so making all items easily accessible from one another makes a purchase of different items that much more tempting.
